facts.claim: The fall of the Western Roman Empire was a centuries-long process of internal decline, not a single event.
Chronic political instability — repeated coups and civil wars — crippled the Western Empire's ability to respond to external threats.
During the Crisis of the Third Century (235–284 CE), at least 26 men were accepted as emperor in roughly 50 years.
The silver content of the denarius fell from over 90 percent in the early empire to about 5 percent by the early 270s CE.
Repeated currency debasement triggered severe inflation across the third-century empire.
Diocletian's Edict on Maximum Prices (301 CE) imposed empire-wide price ceilings enforced by the death penalty, and failed: goods vanished into the black market.
The Antonine Plague (165–180 CE) and the Plague of Cyprian (from 249 CE) caused severe manpower shortages in agriculture and the army.
Estimates place Antonine Plague deaths at roughly 5-10 million people, up to about 10 percent of the empire's population.
facts.notes: Report-only. Modern scholarly estimates vary widely and trace to a narrow scholarly base; needs a second independent estimate before script use.

facts.claim: Estimates of the late Roman army's size range from roughly 400,000 to 600,000 soldiers.
Paying and supplying the army was the largest burden on the imperial budget, and the heavy taxation needed to fund it strained the economy.
The late Western army relied increasingly on barbarian recruits and federate troops whose loyalty lay with their own leaders and their pay, not with Rome.
At the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE, Emperor Valens was killed and much of the eastern field army was destroyed by Gothic forces.
facts.notes: Year reference only; no quantity claim.

facts.claim: After Adrianople, for the first time, entire barbarian peoples were settled inside the empire's borders under their own leaders as federates.
In 395 CE, after the death of Theodosius I — the last emperor to rule the whole empire — the empire was permanently divided between his sons Arcadius (East) and Honorius (West).
Honorius became Western Roman emperor at the age of ten.
In 410 CE the Visigoths under Alaric sacked Rome — the first time in nearly 800 years the city had fallen to a foreign enemy.
Reacting to the sack of Rome, Jerome wrote: "The city which had taken the whole world was itself taken."
By 410 Rome was no longer even the western capital: the court had moved to Milan in 286 and then to Ravenna in 402.
In 439 CE the Vandals under Gaiseric captured Carthage, stripping the Western Empire of North Africa's grain and tax revenue.
As provinces were lost in the 5th century, the Western Empire's tax revenue collapsed, leaving too little money to pay for the army.
In 476 CE the Germanic commander Odoacer deposed the last western emperor, Romulus Augustulus, and no successor was proclaimed; the imperial insignia were sent to Constantinople.
Odoacer's federate soldiers had demanded Italian land to settle on; when the general Orestes refused, they mutinied and made Odoacer their king.
Little changed in Italy immediately after 476: Odoacer kept the Roman administration, the Senate continued to meet, and Latin remained the language of government.
The last western emperor was named after Rome's founder, Romulus; his nickname Augustulus means "little Augustus".
The Eastern Roman Empire survived for nearly 1,000 more years, until Constantinople fell to the Ottomans in 1453.
The empire's land frontiers extended roughly 10,000 km.
facts.notes: No reliable source found within search budget; no frontier-length figure may appear in script or packaging.
